comment_text.sn4.25:1.2: Marcus Bingenheimer proposes emending arati to rati (“delight”), which is in line with the senses of the other names, and is found in certain sources (Studies in Āgama Literature 4.5). However, Sanskrit knows a malignant she-demon arāti, who must be chased away from children (Atharva Veda 118.1). Probably the Chinese translators normalized the term.
comment_text.sn4.25:2.2: Mara’s fascination with the Buddha seems more than professional. He was taking it personally.
comment_text.sn4.25:2.4: Apparently cow elephants were used to trap wild bulls.
comment_text.sn4.25:3.4: At this point, Māra has given up hope.
comment_text.sn4.25:4.3: In this idiom, the locative saṅkhaye acts as an instrumental or ablative of cause (saṅkhayā), that is, “with” or “due to” (rather than “in”) the ending of attachments. In similar contexts, khayā is usually ablative.
comment_text.sn4.25:6.5: They approach an unknown ascetic with an extravagant gesture of submission.
comment_text.sn4.25:14.1: The Buddha’s responds more closely to the accusations than he did with her father. | This verse is quoted and discussed, and the Sutta cited by name as the “Maiden’s Questions” (kumāripañhā) at AN 10.26.
comment_text.sn4.25:16.2: Bodhi notes that the Sanskrit parallels here have present tense tarati for the Pali’s aorist atari. I translate assuming present tense. | Here the five floods must be the five senses, against which the Buddha has proven himself immune. Delight wants to know how to transcend the mind.
comment_text.sn4.25:17.2: The present participle asaṅkharāno is a unique form. The meaning is not entirely clear in this context. The commentary says it means not generating the three kinds of kamma that lead to rebirth.
comment_text.sn4.25:25.1: Like the concluding verse of the previous Sutta, this must have been added by redactors.

root_text.sn4.25:1.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā bhagavato santike imā nibbejanīyā gāthāyo abhāsitvā tamhā ṭhānā apakkamma bhagavato avidūre pathaviyaṁ pallaṅkena nisīdi tuṇhībhūto maṅkubhūto pattakkhandho adhomukho pajjhāyanto appaṭibhāno kaṭṭhena bhūmiṁ vilikhanto.
root_text.sn4.25:1.2: Atha kho taṇhā ca arati ca ragā ca māradhītaro yena māro pāpimā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā māraṁ pāpimantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsiṁsu:
root_text.sn4.25:4.1: Atha kho taṇhā ca arati ca ragā ca māradhītaro yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn4.25:4.3: Atha kho bhagavā na manasākāsi, yathā taṁ anuttare upadhisaṅkhaye vimutto.
root_text.sn4.25:5.1: Atha kho taṇhā ca arati ca ragā ca māradhītaro ekamantaṁ apakkamma evaṁ samacintesuṁ:
root_text.sn4.25:5.3: Yannūna mayaṁ ekasataṁ ekasataṁ kumārivaṇṇasataṁ abhinimmineyyāmā”ti.
root_text.sn4.25:5.4: Atha kho taṇhā ca arati ca ragā ca māradhītaro ekasataṁ ekasataṁ kumārivaṇṇasataṁ abhinimminitvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn4.25:5.6: Tampi bhagavā na manasākāsi, yathā taṁ anuttare upadhisaṅkhaye vimutto.
root_text.sn4.25:6.1: Atha kho taṇhā ca arati ca ragā ca māradhītaro ekamantaṁ apakkamma evaṁ samacintesuṁ:
root_text.sn4.25:6.3: Yannūna mayaṁ ekasataṁ ekasataṁ avijātavaṇṇasataṁ abhinimmineyyāmā”ti.
root_text.sn4.25:6.4: Atha kho taṇhā ca arati ca ragā ca māradhītaro ekasataṁ ekasataṁ avijātavaṇṇasataṁ abhinimminitvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn4.25:6.6: Tampi bhagavā na manasākāsi, yathā taṁ anuttare upadhisaṅkhaye vimutto.
root_text.sn4.25:7.2: yannūna mayaṁ ekasataṁ ekasataṁ sakiṁ vijātavaṇṇasataṁ abhinimmineyyāmāti.
root_text.sn4.25:7.4: sakiṁ vijātavaṇṇasataṁ abhinimminitvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn4.25:7.6: Tampi bhagavā na manasākāsi, yathā taṁ anuttare upadhisaṅkhaye vimutto.
root_text.sn4.25:8.2: yannūna mayaṁ ekasataṁ ekasataṁ duvijātavaṇṇasataṁ abhinimmineyyāmāti.
root_text.sn4.25:9.6: Atha kho taṇhā ca arati ca ragā ca māradhītaro ekamantaṁ apakkamma etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn4.25:11.1: Yañhi mayaṁ samaṇaṁ vā brāhmaṇaṁ vā avītarāgaṁ iminā upakkamena upakkameyyāma hadayaṁ vāssa phaleyya, uṇhaṁ lohitaṁ vā mukhato uggaccheyya, ummādaṁ vā pāpuṇeyya cittakkhepaṁ vā.
root_text.sn4.25:11.2: Seyyathā vā pana naḷo harito luto ussussati visussati milāyati;
root_text.sn4.25:12.1: Atha kho taṇhā ca arati ca ragā ca māradhītaro yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā ekamantaṁ aṭṭhaṁsu.
root_text.sn4.25:12.2: Ekamantaṁ ṭhitā kho taṇhā māradhītā bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn4.25:15.1: Atha kho arati māradhītā bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn4.25:17.4: Na kuppati na sarati na thino.
root_text.sn4.25:19.1: Atha kho ragā māradhītā bhagavato santike gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn4.25:22.1: Atha kho taṇhā ca arati ca ragā ca māradhītaro yena māro pāpimā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu.
root_text.sn4.25:22.2: Addasā kho māro pāpimā taṇhañca aratiñca ragañca māradhītaro dūratova āgacchantiyo.

translation_text.sn4.25:1.1: And then Māra the Wicked, after reciting these verses of disillusionment in the Buddha’s presence, left that place. He sat cross-legged on the ground not far from the Buddha, silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, downcast, depressed, with nothing to say, scratching the ground with a stick.
translation_text.sn4.25:1.2: Then Māra’s daughters Craving, Malignancy, and Lust went up to Māra the Wicked, and addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.25:2.2: What man are you upset about?
translation_text.sn4.25:2.3: We’ll catch him with the snare of lust,
translation_text.sn4.25:2.4: like an elephant in the wild.
translation_text.sn4.25:2.5: We’ll tie him up and bring him back—
translation_text.sn4.25:3.1: “In this world he is the perfected one, the Holy One.
translation_text.sn4.25:3.2: He’s not easily seduced by lust.
translation_text.sn4.25:3.3: He has slipped free of Māra’s dominion;
translation_text.sn4.25:4.1: Then Māra’s daughters Craving, Malignancy, and Lust went up to the Buddha, and said to him,
translation_text.sn4.25:4.3: But the Buddha ignored them, since he was freed with the supreme ending of attachments.
translation_text.sn4.25:5.1: Then Craving, Malignancy, and Lust withdrew to one side to think up a plan.
translation_text.sn4.25:5.2: “Men have a diverse spectrum of tastes.
translation_text.sn4.25:5.3: Why don’t we each manifest in the form of a hundred young maidens?”
translation_text.sn4.25:5.4: So that’s what they did. Then they went up to the Buddha and said to him,
translation_text.sn4.25:5.6: But the Buddha still ignored them, since he was freed with the supreme ending of attachments.
translation_text.sn4.25:6.1: Then Craving, Malignancy, and Lust withdrew to one side to think up a plan.
translation_text.sn4.25:6.2: “Men have a diverse spectrum of tastes.
translation_text.sn4.25:6.3: Why don’t we each manifest in the form of a hundred women who have never given birth?”
translation_text.sn4.25:6.4: So that’s what they did. Then they went up to the Buddha and said to him,
translation_text.sn4.25:6.6: But the Buddha still ignored them, since he was freed with the supreme ending of attachments.
translation_text.sn4.25:7.4: each manifested in the form of a hundred women who have given birth once …
translation_text.sn4.25:8.4: women who have given birth twice …
translation_text.sn4.25:9.5: But the Buddha still ignored them, since he was freed with the supreme ending of attachments.
translation_text.sn4.25:9.6: Then Craving, Malignancy, and Lust withdrew to one side and said,
translation_text.sn4.25:10.1: ‘In this world he is the perfected one, the Holy One.
translation_text.sn4.25:10.2: He’s not easily seduced by lust.
translation_text.sn4.25:10.3: He has slipped free of Māra’s dominion;
translation_text.sn4.25:11.1: For if we had come on to any ascetic or brahmin like this who was not free of lust, his heart would explode, or he’d spew hot blood from his mouth, or he’d go mad or lose his mind.
translation_text.sn4.25:11.2: He’d dry up, wither away, and shrivel up like a green reed that was mowed down.”
translation_text.sn4.25:12.1: Then Māra’s daughters Craving, Malignancy, and Lust went up to the Buddha, and stood to one side.
translation_text.sn4.25:12.2: Māra’s daughter Craving addressed the Buddha in verse:
translation_text.sn4.25:13.1: “Are you swamped by sorrow <j>that you meditate in the forest?
translation_text.sn4.25:13.2: Have you lost a fortune, or do you long for one?
translation_text.sn4.25:13.3: Or perhaps you’ve committed <j>some crime in the village?
translation_text.sn4.25:13.4: Why don’t you get too close to people?
translation_text.sn4.25:13.5: And why does no-one get close to you?”
translation_text.sn4.25:14.1: “I’ve reached the goal, peace of heart.
translation_text.sn4.25:14.2: Having conquered the army <j>of the likable and pleasant,
translation_text.sn4.25:14.3: alone, practicing absorption, I awakened to bliss.
translation_text.sn4.25:14.4: That’s why I don’t get too close to people,
translation_text.sn4.25:14.5: and no-one gets too close to me.”
translation_text.sn4.25:15.1: Then Māra’s daughter Malignancy addressed the Buddha in verse:
translation_text.sn4.25:16.1: “How does a mendicant who has crossed five floods
translation_text.sn4.25:16.2: usually meditate here while crossing the sixth?
translation_text.sn4.25:16.3: How do they usually practice absorption <j>so that sensual perceptions
translation_text.sn4.25:16.4: are blocked out and don’t get hold of them?”
translation_text.sn4.25:17.1: “With tranquil body and mind well freed,
translation_text.sn4.25:17.3: understanding the teaching, <j>they practice absorption without placing the mind;
translation_text.sn4.25:17.4: they’re not shaking or drifting or rigid.
translation_text.sn4.25:18.1: That’s how a mendicant who has crossed five floods
translation_text.sn4.25:18.2: usually meditates here while crossing the sixth.
translation_text.sn4.25:18.3: That’s how they usually practice absorption <j>so that sensual perceptions
translation_text.sn4.25:18.4: are blocked out and don’t get hold of them.”
translation_text.sn4.25:19.1: Then Māra’s daughter Lust addressed the Buddha in verse:
translation_text.sn4.25:20.1: “He lives with his community after cutting off craving,
translation_text.sn4.25:20.2: and many of the faithful will cross over for sure.
translation_text.sn4.25:20.3: Alas, this homeless one will snatch many men away,
translation_text.sn4.25:20.4: and lead them past the King of Death!”
translation_text.sn4.25:21.2: by means of the true teaching.
translation_text.sn4.25:21.3: When the Realized Ones are leading by the teaching,
translation_text.sn4.25:21.4: how could anyone who knows be jealous?”
translation_text.sn4.25:22.1: Then Māra’s daughters Craving, Malignancy, and Lust went up to Māra the Wicked.
translation_text.sn4.25:22.2: Māra the Wicked saw them coming off in the distance,
translation_text.sn4.25:23.3: You dig up a hill with your nails!
translation_text.sn4.25:23.4: You chew iron with your teeth!
translation_text.sn4.25:24.1: You seek a footing in the abyss, as it were,
translation_text.sn4.25:24.2: while lifting a rock with your head!
translation_text.sn4.25:24.3: After attacking a stump with your breast, as it were,
translation_text.sn4.25:25.3: But the Teacher brushed them off right there,
translation_text.sn4.25:25.4: like the gale, a fallen tuft.
translation_text.sn4.25:26.5: The Linked Discourses with Māra are complete.

variant_text.sn4.25:14.3: Ekohaṁ → ekāhaṁ (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, mr)
variant_text.sn4.25:16.3: Kathaṁ jhāyiṁ → kathaṁ jhāyaṁ (sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed); kathajjhāyaṁ (mr)
variant_text.sn4.25:19.1: ragā → rāgā (sya-all); ragā ca (pts1ed, pts2ed, mr)
variant_text.sn4.25:24.1: sirasūhacca → sirasi ūhacca (bj); sirasi ohacca (sya-all, km)
